parts.prompt:
Write the expression for the equilibrium constant, $K_{p}$
, for the forward reaction.
parts.scoring_notes: For the reaction N$_2$(g) + O$_2$(g) ⇌ 2 NO(g): $K_p = \frac{(P_{\text{NO}})^2}{(P_{\text{N}_2})(P_{\text{O}_2})}$. Partial pressures are raised to the power of their stoichiometric coefficients. Pure solids and liquids are omitted from the expression.

scoring_points.description: For a correct $K_p$ expression with pressures raised to stoichiometric coefficients: 1 point

alternatives.correct_example:
$K_p = \frac{(P_{\text{NO}})^2}{(P_{\text{N}_2})(P_{\text{O}_2})}$
NO is squared because the balanced equation has a coefficient of 2 for NO. N$_2$ and O$_2$ each have coefficient 1.

wrong_examples: Writing $K_p = \frac{P_{\text{NO}}}{P_{\text{N}_2} \cdot P_{\text{O}_2}}$ — missing the square on P(NO).
Inverting the expression — products go in the numerator, reactants in the denominator.
common_traps: Stoichiometric coefficients become exponents in the equilibrium expression. NO has coefficient 2, so $P_{NO}$ is squared.
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point is earned for a correct $K_p$ expression.

sample_responses.commentary: Correctly places products over reactants and raises each partial pressure to the power of its stoichiometric coefficient — the CB model answer.
Omits the square on $P_{\text{NO}}$; the coefficient of 2 on NO in the balanced equation becomes an exponent of 2 in the equilibrium expression.

parts.prompt:
A student injects N
(g) and $O_2$(g) into a previously evacuated, rigid vessel and raises the temperature of
the vessel to 2000°C. At this temperature the initial partial pressures of N
(g) and $O_2$(g) are 6.01 atm and
1.61 atm, respectively. The system is allowed to reach equilibrium. The partial pressure of NO(g) at
equilibrium is 0.122 atm. Calculate the value of $K_{p}$
.
Nitrogen monoxide, NO(g), can undergo further reactions to produce acids such as HNO
, a weak acid with a
$K_{a}$
of 4.0 × 10$^{-4}$ and a $pK_a$
of 3.40.

scoring_points.description: For the correct numerical value of $K_p$ using equilibrium pressures: 1 point
wrong_examples: Using initial pressures instead of equilibrium pressures — must subtract the change from N$_2$ and O$_2$.
Forgetting to square P(NO) — the coefficient is 2.
common_traps: ICE table: for every 2 mol NO produced, 1 mol N$_2$ and 1 mol O$_2$ are consumed. Change in P(N$_2$) = change in P(O$_2$) = −P(NO)/2.
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point is earned for the correct equilibrium partial pressures of reactants and products (may be implicit) AND 1 point is earned for the correct calculation of $K_p$. From the ICE table, $2x = 0.122$ atm so $x = 0.0610$ atm; $P_{\text{N}_2} = 6.01 - 0.061 = 5.95$ atm, $P_{\text{O}_2} = 1.61 - 0.061 = 1.55$ atm; $K_p = (0.122)^2/[(5.95)(1.55)] = 0.00161$.

sample_responses.commentary: Correctly uses the stoichiometric ratio (2 mol NO formed per mol N$_2$ and O$_2$ consumed), computes equilibrium partial pressures, and substitutes into $K_p$ — the CB model answer.
Uses initial pressures for N$_2$ and O$_2$ instead of equilibrium pressures; the partial pressures must reflect the small loss (≈ 0.061 atm each) from forming the NO.

parts.prompt:
A student is asked to make a buffer solution with a pH of 3.40 by using 0.100 M HNO
(aq) and
0.100 M NaOH(aq).
(i) Explain why the addition of 0.100 M NaOH(aq) to 0.100 M HNO
(aq) can result in the formation of
a buffer solution. Include the net ionic equation for the reaction that occurs when the student adds
the NaOH(aq) to the HNO
(aq).
(ii) Determine the volume, in mL, of 0.100 M NaOH(aq) the student should add to 100. mL of
0.100 M HNO
(aq) to make a buffer solution with a pH of 3.40. Justify your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: Part (c)(i): NaOH partially neutralizes HNO$_2$ to form NO$_2^−$ (the conjugate base). As long as both HNO$_2$ and NO$_2^−$ remain in solution, a buffer exists. Net ionic equation: OH$^-$(aq) + HNO$_2$(aq) → H$_2$O(l) + NO$_2^-$(aq). Part (c)(ii): Since pK$_a$ = 3.40 = target pH, the Henderson-Hasselbalch equation gives pH = pK$_a$ + log([NO$_2^-$]/[HNO$_2$]) = 3.40 when [NO$_2^-$]/[HNO$_2$] = 1, meaning equal moles. Add 50.0 mL NaOH to convert half of HNO$_2$ to NO$_2^-$.

scoring_points.description: For explaining how a buffer forms from partial neutralization of HNO$_2$: 1 point

required_elements: Adding NaOH partially neutralizes HNO$_2$, producing NO$_2^-$ (conjugate base).
Buffer exists because both HNO$_2$ (weak acid) and NO$_2^-$ (conjugate base) are present in solution.
alternatives.correct_example: A buffer requires a weak acid and its conjugate base. When NaOH is added to HNO$_2$, the reaction OH$^-$ + HNO$_2$ → H$_2$O + NO$_2^-$ converts some HNO$_2$ to NO$_2^-$. As long as both HNO$_2$ and NO$_2^-$ remain (i.e., the NaOH is not added in excess), the solution is a buffer.

wrong_examples: Saying a buffer forms whenever any base is added to an acid — the buffer only forms when both weak acid and conjugate base coexist (partial neutralization only).
common_traps: If NaOH is added in excess (more than equimolar to HNO$_2$), all HNO$_2$ is consumed and there is no longer a buffer.
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point is earned for the recognition that the solution produced is a mixture of a weak acid and its conjugate base.

sample_responses.response: NaOH will neutralize some of the HNO$_2$ to produce NO$_2^-$. The resulting solution contains a mixture of a weak acid and its conjugate base, which is a buffer solution.
sample_responses.commentary: Explicitly names the weak acid (HNO$_2$) and conjugate base (NO$_2^-$) and states that the mixture is what makes the solution a buffer — the CB model answer.

sample_responses.response: Adding NaOH makes the solution basic, which is the defining feature of a buffer.
sample_responses.commentary: A buffer is not defined by being basic — it is defined by having both a weak acid and its conjugate base (or a weak base and its conjugate acid) coexisting in solution.

scoring_points.description: For the correct net ionic equation for NaOH + HNO$_2$: 1 point

alternatives.correct_example:
$\text{OH}^-(\text{aq}) + \text{HNO}_2(\text{aq}) \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}(\text{l}) + \text{NO}_2^-(\text{aq})$
Spectator ions (Na$^+$) are omitted. HNO$_2$ is a weak acid — written as the molecular form in a net ionic equation.

wrong_examples: Writing H$^+$ + OH$^-$ → H$_2$O — this is the net ionic for a strong acid + strong base, not for weak acid neutralization.
Showing Na$^+$ in the equation — Na$^+$ is a spectator ion.
common_traps: Weak acids remain as molecular formulas in net ionic equations (HNO$_2$, not H$^+$ + NO$_2^-$).
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point is earned for the correct net ionic equation: $\text{HNO}_2 + \text{OH}^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{NO}_2^-$.

sample_responses.commentary: Writes HNO$_2$ as a molecular formula (weak acid) and omits spectator Na$^+$ — the CB model answer.
This is the net ionic for a strong acid/strong base neutralization; HNO$_2$ is a weak acid and must appear as the molecular species in its net ionic equation.

scoring_points.description: For identifying that equal moles of HNO$_2$ and NO$_2^-$ are needed when pH = pK$_a$: 1 point

required_elements: Equal moles of HNO$_2$ and NO$_2^-$ required.
alternatives.correct_example: Henderson-Hasselbalch: $\text{pH} = \text{pK}_a + \log\frac{[\text{NO}_2^-]}{[\text{HNO}_2]}$. For pH = pK$_a$ = 3.40: $\log\frac{[\text{NO}_2^-]}{[\text{HNO}_2]} = 0$, so $\frac{[\text{NO}_2^-]}{[\text{HNO}_2]} = 1$. Equal moles (and equal concentrations in the same volume) of HNO$_2$ and NO$_2^-$.

wrong_examples: Using pH = pK$_a$ without recognizing this means a 1:1 ratio of acid to conjugate base.
common_traps: Henderson-Hasselbalch: pH = pKa when [A-]/[HA] = 1. Target pH equals pKa here, so the ratio must equal 1.
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point is earned for clearly indicating a 1-to-1 ratio of HNO$_2$ and NO$_2^-$ (calculation not required). When half of the HNO$_2$ is converted to its conjugate base, $[\text{HNO}_2] = [\text{NO}_2^-]$, so the buffer has pH equal to pK$_a$.

sample_responses.response: From Henderson–Hasselbalch, $\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + \log\frac{[\text{NO}_2^-]}{[\text{HNO}_2]}$. For pH = 3.40 = pK$_a$, the log term must equal 0, which requires $[\text{HNO}_2] = [\text{NO}_2^-]$ — a 1:1 ratio.
sample_responses.commentary: Explicitly identifies the 1:1 ratio as the condition needed when pH equals pK$_a$ — the CB model answer.

sample_responses.response: Add enough NaOH to fully neutralize the HNO$_2$ so the pH equals pK$_a$.
sample_responses.commentary: Full neutralization would produce only NO$_2^-$ — the solution would be a solution of a weak base, not a buffer, and the pH would exceed pK$_a$.

scoring_points.description: For the correct volume of NaOH = 50.0 mL: 1 point

required_elements: Add 50.0 mL of 0.100 M NaOH to 100. mL of 0.100 M HNO$_2$.
This converts exactly half of the HNO$_2$ to NO$_2^-$, giving equal moles of each.
alternatives.correct_example: Moles HNO$_2$ initially = (0.100 M)(0.100 L) = 0.0100 mol. To achieve a 1:1 ratio, convert half → need 0.00500 mol NaOH. Volume NaOH = 0.00500 mol / 0.100 M = 0.0500 L = 50.0 mL.

wrong_examples: Adding 100 mL NaOH — this would completely neutralize all HNO$_2$ with no acid remaining; no buffer formed.
Adding 25.0 mL — this gives a 3:1 HNO$_2$:NO$_2^-$ ratio, not 1:1.
common_traps: The 1:1 ratio means half the acid is neutralized. With 100 mL of 0.100 M HNO$_2$, add exactly 50 mL of 0.100 M NaOH.
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point is earned for the correct volume: 50.0 mL of 0.100 M NaOH(aq).

sample_responses.response: Moles HNO$_2$ initially = $(0.100\text{ M})(0.100\text{ L}) = 0.0100\text{ mol}$. To reach the 1:1 ratio, neutralize half: moles NaOH = $0.00500$ mol. Volume NaOH = $\frac{0.00500\text{ mol}}{0.100\text{ M}} = 0.0500\text{ L} = 50.0\text{ mL}$.
sample_responses.commentary: Shows the calculation connecting the 1:1 ratio requirement to the specific volume of base needed — the CB model answer.

sample_responses.response: Add 100. mL of 0.100 M NaOH — this matches the volume of the HNO$_2$ so the moles are equal.
sample_responses.commentary: Adding equimolar NaOH fully neutralizes all HNO$_2$, leaving only NO$_2^-$ — not a buffer, and the pH would be well above pK$_a$.

parts.prompt:
A second student makes a buffer by dissolving 0.100 mol of NaNO
(s) in 100. mL of 1.00 M HNO
(aq).
Which is more resistant to changes in pH when a strong acid or a strong base is added, the buffer made by
the second student or the buffer made by the first student in part (c) ? Justify your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: The second student's buffer is more resistant to pH change. Buffer capacity is greater when the concentrations of the weak acid and conjugate base are higher. Second student: [HNO$_2$] ≈ 1.00 M (100 mL × 1.00 M = 0.100 mol), [NO$_2^-$] ≈ 1.00 M (0.100 mol in 0.200 L = 0.50 M; or if interpreted as 100 mL total volume with dissolved solid: [NO$_2^-$] ≈ 1.00 M, [HNO$_2$] ≈ 1.00 M). First student: approximately 0.050 M each (after mixing 100 mL + 50 mL). Higher concentration → higher buffer capacity.

scoring_points.description: For identifying the second student's buffer as more resistant with correct concentration-based justification: 1 point

required_elements: The second student's buffer is more resistant to pH changes.
The second student's buffer has higher concentrations of both HNO$_2$ and NO$_2^-$, giving greater buffer capacity.
alternatives.correct_example: The second student's buffer is more resistant to pH change. Buffer capacity depends on the concentrations of the weak acid and conjugate base. The second student's buffer has concentrations ~1.00 M (or 0.50 M) for both HNO$_2$ and NO$_2^-$, while the first student's buffer (50.0 mL 0.100 M NaOH added to 100.0 mL 0.100 M HNO$_2$ = total ~150 mL) has concentrations of about 0.033 M each. Higher concentration means greater buffer capacity.

wrong_examples: Choosing the first student's buffer — the second student's is more concentrated and therefore has higher buffer capacity.
Saying the buffers are equal — they have the same pH but very different concentrations and thus different capacities.
common_traps: Buffer capacity is determined by the molar amounts (or concentrations) of the acid and conjugate base, not just the pH. A buffer at the same pH but higher concentration can absorb more acid or base before the pH changes significantly.
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point is earned for the correct choice (the second student's buffer) and a valid justification: it contains a higher concentration of both HNO$_2$ and NO$_2^-$ available to react with added H$^+$ or OH$^-$ ions.

sample_responses.response: The buffer made by the second student is more resistant to changes in pH because it contains a higher concentration of both HNO$_2$ and NO$_2^-$ available to react with added H$^+$ or OH$^-$ ions.
sample_responses.commentary: Identifies the correct buffer (higher concentration) and cites the greater reserve of weak acid and conjugate base as the reason — the CB model answer.

sample_responses.response: Both buffers resist pH changes equally since they have the same pH.
sample_responses.commentary: Same pH does not mean same buffer capacity — capacity scales with the absolute concentrations of HA and A$^-$, and the second student's buffer has much higher concentrations.

parts.prompt:
A new buffer is made using HNO
(aq) as one of the ingredients. A particulate representation of a small
representative portion of the buffer solution is shown below. (Cations and water molecules are not shown.)
Is the pH of the buffer represented in the diagram greater than, less than, or equal to 3.40 ? Justify your
answer.
parts.scoring_notes: The particulate diagram shows more NO$_2^-$ (conjugate base) than HNO$_2$ (acid). By Henderson-Hasselbalch: pH = 3.40 + log([NO$_2^-$]/[HNO$_2$]). If [NO$_2^-$] > [HNO$_2$], then log([A$^-$]/[HA]) > 0, so pH > 3.40 = pK$_a$.

scoring_points.description: For 'greater than 3.40' with Henderson-Hasselbalch reasoning based on the particulate diagram: 1 point

required_elements: The diagram shows more NO$_2^-$ than HNO$_2$, so [A$^-$]/[HA] > 1, giving pH > pK$_a$ = 3.40.
alternatives.correct_example: pH > 3.40. In the particulate diagram there are more NO$_2^-$ ions than HNO$_2$ molecules, so [NO$_2^-$] > [HNO$_2$]. Henderson-Hasselbalch: $\text{pH} = 3.40 + \log\frac{[\text{NO}_2^-]}{[\text{HNO}_2]}$. Since the log term is positive (ratio > 1), pH > 3.40.

wrong_examples: Saying pH < 3.40 — if more conjugate base than acid is present, the log term is positive and pH exceeds pK$_a$.
Saying pH = 3.40 — that requires equal amounts of acid and conjugate base.
common_traps: Henderson-Hasselbalch: pH > pK$_a$ when [A$^-$] > [HA] (conjugate base predominates). pH < pK$_a$ when [HA] > [A$^-$] (weak acid predominates).
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point is earned for the correct choice AND 1 point is earned for a valid justification. The CB-released particulate diagram shows 10 HNO$_2$ molecules and 5 NO$_2^-$ ions, so $[\text{HNO}_2] > [\text{NO}_2^-]$ and the pH of the buffer is less than 3.40. Using Henderson–Hasselbalch: $\text{pH} = 3.40 + \log(5/10) = 3.10$.

sample_responses.response: If $[\text{HNO}_2] = [\text{NO}_2^-]$, pH = pK$_a$ = 3.40. In the CB-released diagram, there are more HNO$_2$ molecules than NO$_2^-$ ions, so $[\text{HNO}_2] > [\text{NO}_2^-]$ and the pH is less than 3.40. Quantitatively, $\text{pH} = 3.40 + \log(5/10) = 3.10$.
sample_responses.commentary: Interprets the particulate diagram (weak acid predominates), applies Henderson–Hasselbalch, and arrives at the CB answer pH < 3.40.

sample_responses.response: The pH is exactly 3.40 because the question involves a buffer and buffers maintain constant pH.
sample_responses.commentary: A buffer's pH equals pK$_a$ only when the weak acid and conjugate base are equimolar; with more weak acid than conjugate base, the pH is below pK$_a$.
